perturbation.original_text: Integrity will obtain master lease and print agreements with all third party owners of masters embodied in the Product at its own expense. Mechanical rates will not exceed [**]/1/ percent ([**]/1/%) of the then-current statutory rate, and Print licenses will not exceed a prorated portion of [**]/1/ percent ([**]/1/%) of the retail selling price, Integrity will also manufacture the Product and sell to TL its requirements for resale.
perturbation.changed_text: Integrity will endeavor to obtain master lease and print agreements with third party owners of masters embodied in the Product at its own expense. Mechanical rates should generally align with [**]/1/ percent ([**]/1/%) of the then-current statutory rate, and Print licenses are expected to align with a prorated portion of [**]/1/ percent ([**]/1/%) of the retail selling price, Integrity will also manufacture the Product and sell to TL its requirements for resale.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'will obtain' to 'will endeavor' weakens the obligation to secure necessary agreements. Modifying 'will not exceed' to 'should generally align' and 'are expected to align' introduces ambiguity regarding the royalty rates, making adherence discretionary. This allows for potential non-compliance with copyright law.
perturbation.contradicted_law: 17 U.S. Code § 115 - Scope of exclusive rights in nondramatic musical works: Compulsory license for making and distributing phonorecords
perturbation.law_explanation: The modification contradicts 17 U.S. Code § 115 by creating ambiguity around the mechanical royalty rates, which are statutorily defined for compulsory licenses. The law specifies a clear process and rate structure for obtaining mechanical licenses, while the contract now uses vague language that suggests flexibility, potentially resulting in underpayment or non-compliance with compulsory licensing requirements.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 17 U.S. Code § 115 - Scope of exclusive rights in nondramatic musical works: Compulsory license for making and distributing phonorecords | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 17 U.S. Code § 115 - Scope of exclusive rights in nondramatic musical works: Compulsory license for making and distributing phonorecords U.S. Code Notes Authorities (CFR) prev | next In the case of nondramatic musical works, the exclusive rights provided by clauses (1) and (3) of section 106, to make and to distribute phonorecords of such works, are subject to compulsory licensing under the conditions specified by this section. (a) Availability and Scope of Compulsory License in General.— (1) Eligibility for compulsory license.— (A) Conditions for compulsory license.— A person may by complying with the provisions of this section obtain a compulsory license to make and distribute phonorecords of a nondramatic musical work, including by means of digital phonorecord delivery . A person may obtain a compulsory license only if the primary purpose in making phonorecords of the musical work is to distribute them to the public for private use, including by means of digital phonorecord delivery , and— (i) phonorecords of such musical work have previously been distributed to the public in the United States under the authority of the copyright owner of the work, including by means of digital phonorecord delivery ; or (ii) in the case of a digital music provider seeking to make and distribute digital phonorecord deliveries of a sound recording embodying a musical work under a compulsory license for which clause (i) does not apply— (I) the first fixation of such sound recording was made under the authority of the musical work copyright owner, and the sound recording copyright owner has the authority of the musical work copyright owner to make and distribute digital phonorecord deliveries embodying such work to the p
perturbation.justification: The modified contract introduces ambiguities that contradict the clarity required by 17 U.S. Code § 115 regarding mechanical royalty rates. The original contract stated that "Mechanical rates will not exceed [**]/1/ percent ([**]/1/%) of the then-current statutory rate," creating a clear ceiling. The changed text softens this to "Mechanical rates should generally align with [**]/1/ percent ([**]/1/%) of the then-current statutory rate," which creates a legal contradiction by making adherence to the statutory rate discretionary rather than mandatory. This contradicts the purpose of compulsory licensing as described in the scraped snippets, which aim to provide a structured and clear process.

perturbation.original_text: The parties hereby agree that they will mutually share mailing list data of purchasers of the products created hereunder in exchange for names of equal value, (i.e., an expired name for an expired name, active buyer for active buyer.) TL specifically agrees to supply Integrity with data on former subscribers to its "Songs 4 Life" series, who have subsequently canceled for the express purpose of soliciting Product continuity subscriptions.
perturbation.changed_text: The parties may share mailing list data of purchasers of the products created hereunder as they find mutually beneficial, in exchange for names of perceived equal value. TL will consider providing Integrity with data on former subscribers to its "Songs 4 Life" series, who have subsequently canceled.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'they will mutually share' to 'parties may share' and 'TL specifically agrees to supply' to 'TL will consider providing' introduces ambiguity and makes the sharing of mailing list data discretionary. The phrase 'perceived equal value' is also vague. This weakening of the data-sharing obligation could lead to a violation of consumer privacy laws or regulations, particularly if data is not handled with appropriate care.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Consumer Privacy Act (CCPA) Cal. Civ. Code § 1798.100
perturbation.law_explanation: The modification potentially contradicts the CCPA by introducing ambiguity about data sharing practices. If the 'perceived equal value' exchange leads to the sale or transfer of consumer data without proper notice and consent, or if the opt-out rights under CCPA are not respected during the exchange, it could result in a violation. The original text has a stronger requirement of data sharing with 'equal value', which provides better consumer protection than the modified version.

California Consumer Privacy Act of 2018 [1798.100 - 1798.199.100] ( Title 1.81.5 added by Stats. 2018, Ch. 55, Sec. 3. ) 1798.100. General Duties of Businesses that Collect Personal Information (a) A business that controls the collection of a consumer’s personal information shall, at or before the point of collection, inform consumers of the following: (1) The categories of personal information to be collected and the purposes for which the categories of personal information are collected or used and whether that information is sold or shared. A business shall not collect additional categories of personal information or use personal information collected for additional purposes that are incompatible with the disclosed purpose for which the personal information was collected without providing the consumer with notice consistent with this section. (2) If the business collects sensitive personal information, the categories of sensitive personal information to be collected and the purposes for which the categories of sensitive personal information
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: California Consumer Privacy Act (CCPA) | State of California - Department of Justice - Office of the Attorney General Skip to main content California Consumer Privacy Act (CCPA) Home Privacy California Consumer Privacy Act (CCPA) Updated on March 13, 2024 The California Consumer Privacy Act of 2018 (CCPA) gives consumers more control over the personal information that businesses collect about them and the CCPA regulations provide guidance on how to implement the law. This landmark law secures new privacy rights for California consumers, including: The right to know about the personal information a business collects about them and how it is used and shared; The right to delete personal information collected from them (with some exceptions); The right to opt-out of the sale or sharing of their personal information including via the GPC ; The right to non-discrimination for exercising their CCPA rights. In November of 2020, California voters approved Proposition 24, the CPRA , which amended the CCPA and added new additional privacy protections that began on January 1, 2023. As of January 1, 2023, consumers have new rights in addition to those above, such as: The right to correct inaccurate personal information that a business has about them; and The right to limit the use and disclosure of sensitive personal information collected about them. Businesses that are subject to the CCPA have several responsibilities, including responding to consumer requests to exercise these rights and giving consumers certain notices explaining their privacy practices . The CCPA applies to many businesses, including data brokers . CPRA amends the CCPA; it does not create a separate, new law. As a result, our office typically refers to the law as “CCPA” or “CCPA, as amended.” Links to Topics A. GENERAL INFORMATION ABOUT THE CCPA B. RIGHT TO OPT-OUT OF SALE OR SHARING C. REQUESTS TO KNOW D. REQUESTS TO DELETE E. REQUESTS TO CORRECT F. REQUESTS TO LIMIT USE OF PERSONAL INFORMATION G. RIGHT TO NON-DISCRIMINATION H. REQUIRED NOTICES I. DATA BROKERS AND THE CCPA Other Consumer Resources on CCPA Frequently Asked Questions (FAQs) These FAQs provide general consumer information about the CCPA and how you can exercise your rights under the CCPA. They are not legal advice, regulatory guidance, or an opinion of the Attorney General. We will update this information periodically. A. GENERAL INFORMATION ABOUT THE CCPA 1. What rights do I have under the CCPA? If you are a California resident, you may ask businesses to disclose what personal information they have about you and what they do with that information, to delete your personal information, to direct businesses not to sell or share your personal information, to correct inaccurate information that they have about you, and to limit businesses’ use and disclosure of your sensitive personal information: Right to know : You can request that a business disclose to you: (1) the categories and/or specific pieces of personal information they have collected about you, (2) the categories of sources for that personal information, (3) the purposes for which the business uses that information, (4) the categories of third parties with whom the business discloses the information, and (5) the categories of information that the business sells or discloses to third parties. You can make a request to know up to twice a year, free of charge. Right to delete : You can request that businesses delete personal information they collected from you and tell their service providers to do the same, subject to certain exceptions (such as if the business is legally required to keep the information). Right to opt-out of sale or sharing : You may request that businesses stop selling or sharing your personal information (“opt-out”), including via a user-enabled global privacy control. Businesses cannot sell or share your personal information after they receive your opt-out request unless you later authorize them to do so again. Right to correc
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces ambiguity that undermines consumer privacy protections, potentially violating CCPA. By changing 'they will mutually share' to 'parties may share' and 'TL specifically agrees to supply' to 'TL will consider providing,' the obligation to share data becomes discretionary. This contradicts the intent of CCPA, which, as stated in Scraped Law Snippet 1, requires businesses to inform consumers about the categories of personal information collected and the purposes for which it is used. The 'perceived equal value' exchange further weakens this protection, as it introduces a subjective standard that could lead to data transfers without proper notice or consent, going against the consumer rights outlined in Scraped Law Snippet 2, including the right to opt-out of the sharing of personal information. The original text provided a stronger guarantee of data sharing with equal value, offering better consumer protection than the modified version. Therefore, the altered contract introduces Ambiguities that generate a Legal Contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Either party, at its sole expense, upon at least thirty (30) days written notice (and not more than once in respect of any accounting period) will have the right to inspect the other party's books regarding the obligations hereunder for a period of two (2) years from the date on which any statement is rendered. Such auditing party must make specific written objection within such two (2) year period. Thereafter, it will be deemed to have consented to any such statements or accountings which will then be considered an account stated as between the parties, not subject to any objection for any reason whatsoever.
perturbation.changed_text: Either party may, at its discretion and expense, upon what they consider reasonable written notice, inspect the other party's books, potentially, but not obligatorily, regarding the obligations hereunder. The scope and period of such inspection may be subject to further mutual agreement. Any objections must be communicated in a timely manner.
perturbation.explanation: The original obligation to allow inspection is now discretionary ('may, at its discretion'), and the '30 days written notice' is replaced with 'what they consider reasonable written notice', which is subjective. The 'two year' period is removed and replaced by 'may be subject to further mutual agreement'. The definite requirement to make 'specific written objection within such two year period' is removed, and instead it simply states that 'any objections must be communicated in a timely manner'. This alteration significantly weakens the audit rights, making it very difficult to enforce.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Tennessee Code Annotated § 47-9-203 - Attachment and enforceability of security interest; proceeds; supporting obligations; formal requisites
perturbation.law_explanation: While seemingly unrelated at first glance, the weakened audit clause can contradict Tennessee's secured transactions law (TCA § 47-9-203) in the event that royalty payments are used as collateral for a loan. If TL's royalty stream from Integrity is used as collateral, the lender's ability to verify the royalty payments (and therefore the value of the collateral) is severely hampered by the discretionary and vague audit clause. This undermines the lender's ability to ensure the enforceability of their security interest, as it depends on accurate accounting and the ability to verify records which Tennessee law allows for under secured transactions.
perturbation.justification: The original contract provided a clear, enforceable audit right, including specific timelines and objection requirements. The changed text replaces these objective standards with subjective ones, weakening the audit rights to the point of being potentially unenforceable. The obligation to allow inspection is now discretionary, the notice period is 'what they consider reasonable,' and the objection deadline is 'in a timely manner.' This ambiguity undermines the ability to verify royalty payments, especially if used as collateral for a loan, potentially contradicting Tennessee's secured transactions law (TCA § 47-9-203), as described in the law explanation, which highlights the importance of verifying collateral value. Although scraped law snippets are missing, the provided information strongly suggests a contradiction due to the significant weakening of audit rights, fitting the 'Ambiguities Legal Contradiction' definition.
